did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on the DenseNet architecture for the automatic classification of seven classes of skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Benign keratosis, Dermatoﬁbroma, Vascular) from dermoscopic images. It employs a multilevel fine-tuning method with center-loss and an SVM model for final classification using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design and implementation of a novel CNN architecture based on DenseNet with a multilevel fine-tuning method and center-loss. It describes specific methodology, dataset usage (extended HAM10000), training procedures, and reports quantitative results (Precision, Recall, F1-score) for classifying seven skin lesion classes, which constitutes a technical study producing new results rather than a review of prior literature.

title: Classification of Skin Lesions by Combining Multilevel Learnings in a DenseNet Architecture
brief_summary: This study addresses the automatic classification of seven specific skin lesion classes (Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Benign keratosis, Dermatoﬁbroma, and Vascular) using dermoscopic images. The authors propose a novel approach combining a modified DenseNet-62 architecture with a multilevel fine-tuning method and center-loss to generate an ensemble of specialized networks. The model was trained and evaluated on an extended, geometrically augmented version of the HAM10000 dataset containing 10,015 images. Features extracted from the ensemble were concatenated and fed into an SVM classifier with an RBF kernel for final diagnosis. The proposed ensemble approach achieved an average F1-score of 0.82 and a mean precision of 88%, outperforming the original DenseNet-121 and a leading ISIC 2018 challenge solution. The results suggest the method is effective for multi-class skin lesion classification and suitable for deployment in portable diagnostic devices.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. 1,113 images in the training dataset.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion classes (Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Benign keratosis, Dermatoﬁbroma, Vascular)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using dermoscopic images. The model predicts one of seven specific diagnostic categories. The final classification is performed by an SVM classifier using features extracted from an ensemble of DenseNet models. Performance is evaluated using Precision, Recall, and F1-score for each class.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Extended)
datasets_used.description: An extended version of the publicly available HAM10000 dataset, consisting of dermoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The original dataset was augmented with geometric transformations (rotations, flipping, affine) to create a more balanced dataset for training and testing.

use.training: Used for training the DenseNet-62 models via a 5-fold cross-validation approach. The dataset was partitioned into five splits; four splits were used for training in each iteration.
use.testing: Used for testing the models via a 5-fold cross-validation approach. The remaining one split out of five was used for testing in each iteration.
use.other: The original, non-augmented HAM10000 dataset served as the base for the extended dataset used in this study. The study also references the ISIC 2018 challenge dataset (which is based on HAM10000) for comparative purposes.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To partition the HAM10000 dataset into training and testing splits for model evaluation and to ensure robust performance estimation across different data subsets.
To evaluate and compare the classification performance of the proposed ensemble model, individual fine-tuned models, and the baseline DenseNet-121 across seven skin lesion classes.
To visualize the distribution of correct and incorrect predictions for each class in the ensemble classifier.
To perform dimensional reduction on the concatenated feature vectors extracted from the ensemble of networks before training the SVM classifier.

statistical_methods_used.method: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with RBF kernel
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To perform the final multi-class classification using the reduced feature vectors from the ensemble of CNNs.
To minimize intra-class variance and maximize inter-class separation during the CNN training phase by balancing with softmax loss.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using Precision, Recall, F1-score, and Confusion Matrices via 5-fold cross-validation, with comparisons made against baseline models and ISIC 2018 challenge results based on these metrics.

HAM10000 (Extended).notes: The proposed ensemble approach achieved an average F1-score of 0.82 and mean precision of 88%, outperforming the baseline DenseNet-121 (Average F1: 0.30, Precision: 0.49) and the best solution from the ISIC 2018 challenge (Mean Precision: 82.6%). For the ensemble model, F1-scores were greater than 0.8 for all classes except Melanoma (0.72) and Keratosis (0.62).

architecture_details.modifications: First two Transition Layers and first two Dense Blocks maintained; number of layers in the third Dense Block reduced; total layers 62

training_details.learning_rate: Starts at 0.01, decreased by factor of 10 at each step of 20000 iterations
training_details.loss_function: Linear combination of Softmax loss and Center-loss (L = Ls + γLc)
training_details.fine_tuning_strategy: Multilevel fine-tuning on the last Dense Block. Six models generated by fine-tuning different numbers of the last convolution layers of the third Dense Block (last 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13 layers respectively) in separate sessions.

classification_or_output_head.details: Probability outputs from six fine-tuned DenseNet-62 models were concatenated into a single feature vector. Dimensionality reduction was performed using PCA. The reduced features were fed into a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier with an RBF kernel (λ = 0.01, C = 10) for final classification.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with RBF kernel
preprocessing_techniques: Geometric transformations for data augmentation: rotations, flipping, and affine transformations applied to the HAM10000 dataset to obtain a more balanced training set.
Centered cropping: each image was squared by centered cropping with an amplitude equal to the shorter side of the starting image.
Resizing: resulting patches were resized to 224 x 224 pixels to match the network input layer.
Dimensionality reduction: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) applied to concatenated feature vectors extracted from the ensemble of networks before training the SVM classifier.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotations, flipping, and affine transformations were applied to the HAM10000 dataset images to obtain a more balanced training set and increase the number of samples for each class.
Each image was squared by centered cropping with an amplitude equal to the shorter side of the starting image.
Resulting patches were resized to 224 x 224 pixels to match the network input layer.

conclusions: The proposed method, which combines a modified DenseNet-62 architecture with a multilevel fine-tuning strategy and an SVM classifier, achieved an average F1-score of 0.82 and a mean precision of 88% on the extended HAM10000 dataset.
The ensemble approach outperformed the baseline DenseNet-121 model, which achieved a significantly lower average F1-score of 0.30 and mean precision of 0.49.
The proposed ensemble approach demonstrated superior performance compared to the best solution from the ISIC 2018 challenge (PNASNet Ensemble), which reported a mean precision of 82.6% ± 2.0%.
The DenseNet-121 baseline showed poor generalization capabilities, attributed to its high complexity and the insufficient data augmentation for the relatively small dataset size.
The multilevel fine-tuning method generated specialized networks that performed better on specific classes, justifying the use of an ensemble rather than a single end-to-end classifier.
The F1-scores for the ensemble model were greater than 0.8 for all classes except Melanoma (0.72) and Keratosis (0.62).
The use of reduced models derived from the same architecture makes the approach suitable for deployment in embedded systems and portable devices for domestic diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single dataset (extended HAM10000) with no external validation, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The dataset is highly imbalanced (e.g., 6,705 Melanocytic nevi vs. 115 Dermatofibromas), which may bias the model towards majority classes despite augmentation.
Performance on Melanoma (F1: 0.72) and Actinic Keratosis (F1: 0.62) is notably lower than other classes, raising concerns about reliability for critical malignant diagnoses.

The comparison with the ISIC 2018 challenge solution (PNASNet) is indirect; the authors note that no published work explicitly exploits the HAM10000 dataset at the time, making direct benchmarking difficult.

The baseline DenseNet-121 performed poorly (Average F1: 0.30), attributed to high complexity and insufficient data augmentation for the dataset size, suggesting the proposed method's success is partly due to architectural simplification.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (p-values) for the performance differences between the proposed ensemble and baselines.

Data leakage risk exists if the geometric augmentations (rotation, flipping, affine) are not strictly applied per-split in the 5-fold cross-validation, though the text implies splits were created before augmentation.
The model was trained on dermoscopic images only; clinical relevance for non-dermoscopic or clinical photography is unverified.
The authors acknowledge the need for future work on color constancy and segmentation, indicating current preprocessing limitations.
